content.text: Claude's vision and document capabilities allow agents to reason over images, PDFs, and text files alongside conversational messages. File handling in an agentic application involves accepting uploads, validating them, storing them safely, and encoding them in the format the API expects.
type handles multipart form data. Validate MIME type and file size before reading the bytes.

import mimetypes, hashlib
from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile, File, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()

ALLOWED_TYPES = {
    "image/jpeg", "image/png", "image/gif", "image/webp",
    "application/pdf",
    "text/plain", "text/csv", "text/markdown",
    "application/json",
}
MAX_BYTES = 32 * 1024 * 1024  # 32 MB (Claude's PDF limit)

@app.post("/upload")
async def upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    # Read in chunks to enforce size limit without loading full file
    content = b""
    async for chunk in file:
        content += chunk
        if len(content) > MAX_BYTES:
            raise HTTPException(413, f"File exceeds {MAX_BYTES // 1024 // 1024} MB limit")

    # Verify MIME from magic bytes, not just filename
    import magic
    detected = magic.from_buffer(content[:4096], mime=True)
    if detected not in ALLOWED_TYPES:
        raise HTTPException(415, f"Unsupported file type: {detected}")

    file_hash = hashlib.sha256(content).hexdigest()
    return {"size": len(content), "mime": detected, "sha256": file_hash}
(which reads file magic bytes) rather than trusting the Content-Type header or filename extension. Both can be spoofed.

Claude accepts images embedded as base64 data URIs or referenced by URL (for images hosted publicly). For user-uploaded images, always embed them as base64 — external URLs may expire or become inaccessible.
Image blocks must appear in the message
list alongside text blocks. You can include multiple images in a single message — Claude reads them all in the order they appear.
block pattern as images but with type
. Claude reads the extracted text from all pages.

Text-based files (CSV, Markdown, code) do not use the document block — read their contents and include them directly in the prompt text. This preserves formatting context (e.g., CSV column headers) without relying on PDF extraction.
For multi-turn conversations, store uploaded files in S3 rather than re-embedding them in every API call. Pass a presigned URL or cached content in subsequent turns.

import boto3, uuid, base64
from botocore.config import Config

s3 = boto3.client("s3", config=Config(signature_version="s3v4"))
BUCKET = "agent-attachments"

def upload_to_s3(file_bytes: bytes, mime: str, user_id: str) -> str:
    key = f"uploads/{user_id}/{uuid.uuid4()}"
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=BUCKET,
        Key=key,
        Body=file_bytes,
        ContentType=mime,
        ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
    )
    return key

def load_attachment_as_base64(key: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
    obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key)
    body = obj["Body"].read()
    mime = obj["ContentType"]
    return base64.standard_b64encode(body).decode(), mime

def presign_attachment(key: str, expires: int = 3600) -> str:
    return s3.generate_presigned_url(
        "get_object",
        Params={"Bucket": BUCKET, "Key": key},
        ExpiresIn=expires,
    )
Store the S3 key in the session or conversation record. For image/PDF turns, fetch the bytes and embed as base64. For display in the UI, generate a presigned URL that expires after one hour.
DOCX, XLSX, and audio/video files are not natively supported by Claude. Convert them to a supported format before passing to the API.

import subprocess, tempfile, pathlib

def docx_to_text(docx_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".docx", delete=False) as tmp:
        tmp.write(docx_bytes)
        tmp_path = tmp.name
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["pandoc", "--to", "plain", tmp_path],
        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30,
    )
    pathlib.Path(tmp_path).unlink(missing_ok=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}")
    return result.stdout

def xlsx_to_csv(xlsx_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    import openpyxl, csv, io
    wb = openpyxl.load_workbook(io.BytesIO(xlsx_bytes), read_only=True, data_only=True)
    ws = wb.active
    out = io.StringIO()
    writer = csv.writer(out)
    for row in ws.iter_rows(values_only=True):
        writer.writerow([str(v) if v is not None else "" for v in row])
    return out.getvalue()
Browsers send the MIME type the OS guesses from the filename extension. Verify with python-magic before accepting the file.
Re-embedding large files on every turn.
A 20 MB PDF embedded as base64 adds ~27 MB of text to every API call in the conversation. Store in S3 after the first turn and re-fetch when needed.
Claude cannot read DOCX format. The bytes will be treated as binary noise. Always convert to plain text or PDF first.
Claude supports up to 100 pages. A 300-page PDF will be silently truncated. Count pages with PyPDF2 before sending and warn the user if needed.
Storing raw file bytes in Redis.
Redis is a poor fit for large binary blobs — it stores everything in memory. Always use S3 or a blob store for files and keep only the S3 key in Redis.
A single user turn can include multiple files alongside text. Build the content list programmatically when the number of attachments varies.

Interleave text blocks between file blocks to give the model context about each file's purpose. Without labels, the model may conflate the files or lose track of which content belongs to which document.
Claude supports up to 100 pages per PDF. Check before sending to give users a clear error message rather than silently truncated output.
In multi-turn conversations, users sometimes re-upload the same file. Detect duplicates by hashing the content and reuse the S3 key rather than storing and re-encoding the same bytes.

import hashlib, redis.asyncio as redis

async def get_or_store_attachment(
    r: redis.Redis,
    s3_client,
    file_bytes: bytes,
    mime: str,
    user_id: str,
    bucket: str,
) -> str:
    content_hash = hashlib.sha256(file_bytes).hexdigest()
    cache_key = f"attach:{user_id}:{content_hash}"

    existing_key = await r.get(cache_key)
    if existing_key:
        return existing_key.decode()

    # Not seen before — store in S3
    import uuid
    s3_key = f"uploads/{user_id}/{uuid.uuid4()}"
    s3_client.put_object(
        Bucket=bucket,
        Key=s3_key,
        Body=file_bytes,
        ContentType=mime,
        ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
    )
    # Cache the mapping for 7 days
    await r.set(cache_key, s3_key, ex=7 * 86400)
    return s3_key

import pytest, io

def test_validate_image_type():
    import magic
    png_magic = b"\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n"
    # fake minimal PNG header
    fake_png = png_magic + b"\x00" * 100
    detected = magic.from_buffer(fake_png, mime=True)
    assert detected == "image/png"

def test_xlsx_to_csv_roundtrip():
    import openpyxl
    wb = openpyxl.Workbook()
    ws = wb.active
    ws.append(["Name", "Score"])
    ws.append(["Alice", 95])
    ws.append(["Bob", 87])
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    wb.save(buf)
    csv_output = xlsx_to_csv(buf.getvalue())
    assert "Name,Score" in csv_output
    assert "Alice,95" in csv_output

def test_pdf_page_check_raises_over_limit():
    import pypdf
    # Build a 2-page PDF in memory
    writer = pypdf.PdfWriter()
    for _ in range(2):
        writer.add_blank_page(width=612, height=792)
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    writer.write(buf)
    # Should raise because max_pages=1
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="2 pages"):
        check_pdf_pages(buf.getvalue(), max_pages=1)

def test_multifile_message_structure():
    tiny_png = (
        b"\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n" + b"\x00" * 50
    )
    msg = build_multifile_message(
        text="Describe this.",
        attachments=[(tiny_png, "image/png", "img.png")],
    )
    assert msg["role"] == "user"
    image_blocks = [b for b in msg["content"] if b.get("type") == "image"]
    assert len(image_blocks) == 1
    assert image_blocks[0]["source"]["type"] == "base64"
File handling in agentic applications follows a four-step pipeline:
MIME type and size with python-magic,
in S3 for multi-turn use, encode as
unsupported formats (DOCX, XLSX) to text before sending. Text files bypass the binary pipeline and are inlined directly into the prompt.
